coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots decorated hierarchical litchi-like In2O3 nanospheres for highly sensitive and selective NO2 detection
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Ming
Wu, Zepei
Liu, Guannan
Zhao, Lianjing
Gao, Yuan
Li, Shan
Zhang, Bo
Yan, Xu
Geyu, Lu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots, being one of the zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterials, possess numerous fancy characteristics in physics and chemistry, such as quantum size, abundant edges and functional groups, and high electrical conductivity, which are beneficial to gas sensing properties. It also has the advantages of low-cost and environmental-friendly preparation. In this work, carbon dots were employed as dopants for modification of hierarchical litchi-like In2O3 nanospheres via a facile and environment-friendly hydrothermal approach to improve the gas sensing properties. As-obtained products were characterized by X-ray diffraction, field emission scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, etc. Gas sensing properties of In2O3/carbon dots and pristine In2O3 were investigated systematically. As expected, the sensors based on the In2O3/carbon dots displayed enhanced response at working temperature of 50 °C, which was 4 times as high as the sensors based on the pristine In2O3. The sensors presented excellent selectivity. The enhancement of sensing properties should be mainly attributed to the heterojunctions constructed across the interface between the In2O3 and carbon quantum dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal oxide
Carbon dots
Gas sensor
In2O3
Hybrids

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of In2O3 /carbon dots and In2O3 nanospheres
Sensor production and gas sensing test
Chemical composition and chemical state configuration
Thermal gravimetric and differential scanning calorimeter analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Wide-angle XRD patterns of In2O3 and In2O3/carbon dots, (b) enlarged pattern of peak (222).
(a–b) Typical SEM images of hierarchical In2O3, (c) Typical TEM image of In2O3, (d) Typical HRTEM image of In2O3.
(a) Typical HR-TEM and TEM images of Carbon dots, (b–c) Typical SEM images of hierarchical In2O3/carbon dots nanospheres. (d) Typical HRTEM image of an In2O3/carbon dots nanospheres.
(a) In2O3 dots sample, (b–d) its EDS elemental mapping images of C, In, and O, respectively. (e) In2O3 /carbon sample, (f–h) its EDS elemental mapping images of C, In, and O, respectively.
The survey scan of XPS spectra of (a) In2O3, (b) In2O3/carbon dots, (c–d) O 1s profiles, (e–f) In 3d profiles.
(a) Responses towards NO2 at various working temperature, (b) Responses of pure In2O3 and In2O3/carbon dots to gases (500 ppb) at 50℃.
(a) Dynamic response curve of the sensor based on the In2O3 a and In2O3/carbon dots 50℃ respectively, (b) Response toward different NO2 concentration at 50℃.
(a) Repeated response-recovery curves to 500 ppb NO2 at 50 °C, (b) Long-term stability of the gas sensor based on the In2O3/carbon dots to 500 ppb NO2 at 50 °C.
Sensor response of the gas sensor based on the In2O3/carbon dots to 500 ppb NO2 gas in the presence of different relative humidity at 50℃.
Schematic diagram of the sensing mechanism for the enhanced response to NO2.
Comparison of sensing performance to NO2 between present work and reported literature.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots decorated hierarchical litchi-like In2O3 nanospheres for highly sensitive and selective NO2 detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, Jilin Province Key Laboratory on Gas Sensors, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics
Jilin Province Key Laboratory on Gas Sensors
College of Electronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, Jilin Province Key Laboratory on Gas Sensors, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots, being one of the zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterials, possess numerous fancy characteristics in physics and chemistry, such as quantum size, abundant edges and functional groups, and high electrical conductivity, which are beneficial to gas sensing properties. It also has the advantages of low-cost and environmental-friendly preparation. In this work, carbon dots were employed as dopants for modification of hierarchical litchi-like In2O3 nanospheres via a facile and environment-friendly hydrothermal approach to improve the gas sensing properties. As-obtained products were characterized by X-ray diffraction, field emission scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, etc. Gas sensing properties of In2O3/carbon dots and pristine In2O3 were investigated systematically. As expected, the sensors based on the In2O3/carbon dots displayed enhanced response at working temperature of 50 °C, which was 4 times as high as the sensors based on the pristine In2O3. The sensors presented excellent selectivity. The enhancement of sensing properties should be mainly attributed to the heterojunctions constructed across the interface between the In2O3 and carbon quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid expansion of industry, the emission of poisonous and harmful gases has drawn people's attention increasingly owing to their severe threat to human health [1,2]. As a typical hazardous gas, nitrogen dioxide (NO2) mainly comes from the use of detergent fossil fuels, vehicle exhaust and so on [3–5]. The gas can make us cough, irritate our eyes, cause dizziness. More than that, it usually weakens our respiratory system chronically [6,7]. Low NO2 concentration (10 − 20 ppm) can induce irritation in nose and eyes, and concentration higher than 100 ppm may cause death due to the asphyxia. Therefore, it is greatly significant for public health to develop highly sensitive and reliable NO2 gas sensors.

Owing to the advantage of high sensitivity, low cost and easy fabrication, sensors based on the oxide semiconductors have attracted wide attention and extensive research [8,9]. Over the past few decades, lots of gas sensors based on the metal oxide semiconductors have been reported, such as ZnO [10,11], SnO2 [12,13], In2O3 [14,15], NiO [16,17], WO3 [18,19], and Co3O4 [20,21]. Among the various gas sensing materials, n-type semiconductor In2O3 with wide band gap, has been extensively researched owing to its high electrical conductivity. In2O3 exhibited electrical resistance response in NO2 atmosphere. However, pure In2O3 still suffer from some drawbacks resulting from their intrinsic physical or chemical characters, for instance, confined sensitivity and poor selectivity, which will hinder their further development undoubtedly [22,23].

The morphology and architecture of metal oxide are of great importance to its sensing performance. Several strategies including mesoporous structure [24], hollow microsphere and hierarchical structure [25] have been explored for high gas sensor with excellent performance. These architectures provide small primary grain of metal oxide, large surface area with abundant active sites and unobstructed diffusion pathway for gas, which all benefit to improvement of gas-sensing properties. Among these architectures, hierarchical metal oxide could be easily obtained by one-step hydrothermal procedure. Furthermore, the strategies of modification on the interface of metal oxide are developed, including precious metal loading [26], heteroatom doping and local heterojunction construction [27]. Last decade, p-n heterojunction has arose interest of researchers progressively owing to electrical conductivity modulation effect and the synergistic effect from two semiconductor components [28,29], which are proved as effective way to enhance sensing performance substantially.

Carbonaceous materials have recently attracted significant attention from researchers in various fields. The researches about heterojunction between carbonaceous materials and In2O3 has already aroused the concern of sensors field and produced a large number of research results. Zhang et al. have reported that In2O3/multi-walled carbon nanotube nanocomposites show much higher response to ethanol [30]. Ellis et al. have obtained In2O3/single walled carbon nanotube hybrid, which demonstrates enhancement in sensing characteristic [31]. Liu and co-workers have developed In2O3/rGO nanocomposite with flower-like morphology showing highly sensitive NO2 sensing performance [32]. Compared with other carbonaceous materials (CNTs, GO, rGO), carbon dots as a novel promising functional material possess some unique properties and convenient synthesis procedure. It has zero-dimensional size with diameter usually below 10 nm. It consist of graphite core or amorphous carbon, and there are plenty of edges/function groups with high activity on its surface [33]. Moreover, the electronic transmission can also be modulated as carbon dots can serve as carrier reservoirs in heterojunction [34]. Notably, carbon dots are easily prepared via simple hydrothermal route starting from small molecules. The synthesis of carbon dots is mild and environmentally friendly, avoiding strong oxidation treatment procedure and strongly acid waste produced. On the basis of investigation of other carbonaceous material, we have reasons to deduce that the carbon dots with quantum size can also be promising dopants for metal oxide-based chemiresistor sensors owing to its unique physical and chemical characteristic [35–37].

Herein, uniform hierarchical litchi-like In2O3/carbon dots nanospheres were synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal method combined with the subsequent annealing process. As a proof-of-concept, gas sensors based on the as-prepared In2O3/carbon dots were fabricated and their gas sensing performance was investigated. As expected, the as-prepared sensors exhibited an ultrahigh response for the detection of NO2 at a relative low working temperature (50 °C). Furthermore, the theoretical detection limit was as low as 27 ppt with excellent selectivity and stability. The sensors based on the In2O3/carbon dots nanaospheres could be an excellent candidate for NO2 detection.

Indium chloride tetra hydrate (InCl3·4H2O, ≥ 97.0 %) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation (Shanghai, China). Urea (CH4N2O, ≥ 99.0 %) and citric acid (C6H8O7, ≥ 99.5 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent co., Ltd, China. All the chemicals used in the experiment were of analytical grade and used as received. Deionized water and absolute ethanol (C2H6O, 99.9 %) were used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of In2O3 /carbon dots and In2O3 nanospheres
ce:section.ce:para: In2O3 nanospheres and In2O3/carbon dots nanospheres were prepared by a hydrothermal route. In a typical process, 0.6 g urea(CH4N2O) were dissolved into 20 mL deionized water to form a homogeneous solution, which was marked as solution A. Simultaneously, 0.3 g InCl3·4H2O and 0.4 g citric acid (C6H8O7) were added into 20 mL deionized water under continuous stirring to form solution B. Then, solution A was slowly added to solution B to form mixed solution under magnetic stirring. After constant stirring for 30 min, the resultant solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel auto-clave and maintained the temperature at 120 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave cooled to room temperature naturally, the as-prepared precipitates were collected by centrifugation, washed several times with deionized water and absolute ethanol. After dried in a 60 °C oven, the white precipitates were loaded into an alumina boat, heated to 350 °C at a heating rate of 2 °C/min in a furnace, and calcined at that temperature for 2 h in the nitrogen atmosphere. Afterwards the In2O3/carbon dots nanospheres were obtained. Similarly, the In2O3 nanospheres were obtained by calcination in air while other condition unchanged.

The crystal phase was analyzed by X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD; Rigaku D/Max 2550) using Cu Kα radiation (λ =1.5418 Å). XPS measurement was carried out by an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using an X-ray source (Al Kα hυ =1486.6 eV). The detailed microstructure was observed by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM; JEOL JSM-7500 F), transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEOL JSM-2100 F), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; JEOL JSM-2100 F). The surface area and pore-size distribution were measured by Brunauer − Emmett − Teller (BET) and Barrett − Joyner−Halenda (BJH) methods using N2 adsorption − desorption isotherms (Micrometrics Gemini VII). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a Thermogravimetric Analyzer (Netzsch STA 499 F3. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra was recorded using a RF-5301PC (Shimadzu, Japan) fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensor production and gas sensing test
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we mixed a certain amount of samples with ethanol, which was followed by grinding until the homogeneous slurry was obtained. Secondly, the paste was coated on the ceramic tube with two gold electrodes to form a thick sensing film, which was crucial to the gas sensing. Third, a Ni − Cr alloy coil was inserted into the ceramic tube as a heater to control the operation temperature. Afterwards, the ceramic tube and Ni − Cr alloy coil were welded onto a plastic base. Finally, the as-prepared product was aged at 200 °C for at least 2 days. The gas sensing properties was investigated by a static testing system (30 ± 5 %RH). The gas response (S) was defined as the ratio of Rg/Ra (oxiding gas, such as Cl2), where Ra and Rg were the electrical resistance of sensor in air and testing gas environment, respectively. The sensor resistance was measured by Fluke 8846A 6–112 DIGIT precision multimeter with the upper limit of 1150 MΩ.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed to analyze the crystalline phase of the as-prepared samples. Fig. 1 (a) demonstrated the XRD patterns of In2O3 and the In2O3/carbon dots hybrid. As depicted, both the pure In2O3 and the In2O3/carbon dots hybrid displayed the similar diffraction peaks and could be perfectly indexed to the cubic In2O3. The strong diffraction peaks at 21.4°, 30.5°, 35.4° and 50.9° were consistent with the (211), (222), (400) and (440) planes of the cubic In2O3 (JCPDS no.65–3170) [38]. In addition, an evident swell at around 20°∼25°, which was a representative of carbon material, was found in the XRD pattern of In2O3/carbon dots composite compared with the pristine In2O3 [39]. Although the lattice fringe of carbon dots were observed in HRTEM images, its low graphitization degree, low percentage content, small size, and good dispersion in hybrid disturbed the observation of its weak diffraction peak and caused emerging of the swell in the XRD pattern. Fig. 2 (b) displayed the enlarged pattern of (222) peak of both samples. With the presence of carbon dots, the 2θ values decreased from 30.64 to 30.52, which could be attributed to some carbon incorporation into the matrix of In2O3 across the interface of In2O3 and carbon dot due to the close contact with each other.

The morphologies of the as-prepared samples were explored by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 2 showed the electron micrographs of In2O3 nanospheres. As shown in Fig. 2(a), numerous highly well-regulated spheres with uniform size were readily identified. The single In2O3 nanosphere was observed to be in the size of about 110 nm with rough surface. TEM was then employed to gain further insight into the In2O3 nanostructures. As showed in Fig. 2(c), we can found that a batch of primary nanoparticles integrated together to form a litchi-like hierarchical sphere with rough surface. Then the HRTEM image (Fig. 2(d)) displayed a lattice plane spacing of ∼0.292 nm, which could be attributed to the distance between the (222) crystal planes of cubic In2O3 [40].

Before investigation of In2O3/carbon dots hybrid, we employed the typical synthesis procedure to obtain carbon dots. The experimental condition was as same as that for In2O3/carbon dots hybrid but at the absence of InCl3·4H2O. TEM and HRTEM images of the carbon dots were showed in the Fig. 3 (a). As can be seen, the carbon dots possessed uniform diameter calculated to be around 5 nm. HRTEM image (inset image of Fig. 3(a)) displayed a lattice plane spacing of ∼0.21 nm corresponding to the distance between the (100) crystal planes of sp2 carbon material [41].

Then we investigated thoroughly the morphologies and interior structure of In2O3/carbon dots hybrid. Fig. 3(c) showed the electron micrographs of In2O3/carbon dots. In2O3/carbon dots hybrids almost entirely retained the same morphology and size as pristine In2O3. The HRTEM images shown in Fig. 3(d) presented distinct fringes with plane spacing of 0.292 and 0.413 nm, which could be attributed to (222) and (211) crystal planes of cubic In2O3, respectively. In addition, we can also observe fringes with plane spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) crystal planes of sp2 carbon material, which can manifest the presence of carbon dots in hybrids. The area where the lattice fringes of In2O3 and carbon dots overlapped with each other were distinguishable, which demonstrating the formation of heterojunctions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical composition and chemical state configuration
ce:section.ce:para: To ascertain the chemical composition of the In2O3 and In2O3/carbon dots samples, the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was employed, as shown in Figure S1. Si and B were from the silicon substrate, and Pt was derived from the platinum coating layer used in measurement process. The C signal could be detected in both two samples, but the intensity of C signal in In2O3/carbon dot was obviously stronger than one in the In2O3. The traces of carbon in In2O3 sample could be derived from the residual of raw materials. The EDS elemental mapping was carried out to directly observe the distribution of elements in the samples. As can be seen from Fig. 4 , In, O, and C elements distributed intensively around the samples. Notably, owing to the grid membrane used in the experiment, the carbon element as inevitable background was also concentrated in the upper right corner of carbon element mapping of both samples, as shown in the Fig. 4(b) and (f). Besides that, we could observe C elements distributed as a nanosphere shape in the image of the hybrid sample (Fig. 4(f)), which was in accordance with In and O elements. The results indicated carbon dots dispersed uniformly in the hybrid.

Raman analysis has been conducted on the In2O3 and In2O3/carbon dots as Figure S2 showed. The value of ID/IG showed that the carbon dot contains lots of defects, which could derived from the abundant edges, multiple functional groups and the interaction between carbon dot and In2O3 particles. The result can confirm the existence of carbon material with abundant defects in In2O3/carbon dots samples.

The fluorescence spectra in Figure S3 can further prove the carbon in the hybrid was carbon dots. Under the light excitement with wavelength of 280 nm, the carbon dots exhibited a strong fluorescence emission at 445 nm. However, although the fluorescence of hybrid quenched largely than that of pure carbon dots, the intensity was still strong in contrast to that of the pristine In2O3. The remained fluorescence of In2O3/carbon dots hybrid demonstrated the existence of carbon dots in hybrid, and the fluorescence quenching owing to carrier transfer between two components of hybrid indirectly verified the formation of heterojunction at the interfaces.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were employed to investigate the elemental composition and bonding configurations of samples. Fig. 5 (a, b) illustrated the survey scan of the XPS spectra of the In2O3 and In2O3/carbon dots. As can be seen, the In2O3/carbon dots samples consisted of In, O and C without any other impurity. Owing to the carbon contamination for the measurement calibration, a relative weaker C peak also appeared in the pattern of pure In2O3. As displayed in the Fig. 5(c, d), the high resolution O 1s spectra could be fitted by three peaks with binding energy at 529.7 eV, 530.2 eV and 531.6 eV, which could be assigned to lattice oxygen (OL), oxygen vacancy (OV) within the matrix of crystal, and surface adsorption oxygen (OS) respectively [42]. The relative percentage of OL, OV, and OS in In2O3 was calculated to be about 47, 34, and 19.0 %. In contrast, in the case of In2O3/carbon dots, the corresponding relative percentage was approximately 27, 46, and 27 %, respectively. The proportion of OS and OV in the In2O3/carbon dots hybrid significantly increased. It was acknowledged that the surface absorbed oxygen species exerted positive effects in the enhancement of gas sensing performances owing to their capability of reacting with test gas molecules. Thus, the sensing properties of In2O3/carbon dots hybrid would be improved potentially. As shown in Figure S4, the C1 s of In2O3/carbon samples can be fitted into CO (288.9 eV), CC (284.7 eV), C–O (285.4 eV) and CC (283.6 eV). And the C1 s of In2O3 samples can be fitted into CO (288.9 eV), CC (284.6 eV), and C–O (285.5 eV) [43,44]. C–O, and CO existed in In2O3, which could be attributed to the residual of raw materials. The CC bond only existed in the In2O3/carbon samples, which could be attributed to the sp2 C in the carbon dots. In addition, the weak intensity of CC also indicated a low graphitization degree of the carbon dots. As Fig. 5(e, f) displayed, peak position of In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2 were located at 444.45 eV and 452.00 eV for In2O3. In contrast, peak position of In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2 were located at 444.25 eV and 451.85 eV for In2O3/carbon dot, which both moved to a lower energy direction, indicating that the chemical environment of indium had changed. The reason was that the chemisorption oxygen interacted with indium atom and weakened the bond of In and lattice oxygen. The electron density around indium atom would increase, and then enhanced the shielding effect.

In a general way, surface area was one of the most important indicators to evaluate a functional material. BET surface area and pore-size distribution were explored by nitrogen adsorption − desorption measurement. The BET surface areas of In2O3 and In2O3/Carbon dots products were calculated to be 60 and 63 m2 g−1 respectively, which were fairly high and played a positive role in enhancing the gas-sensing performance. As showed in the pore size distribution plots in the inset of Figure S5, the pore size in both the as-prepared products was around 2 − 30 nm. Notably, owing to that the In2O3 and In2O3/Carbon dots almost possessed entirely same morphology and structure, no remarkable difference was found between the two products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal gravimetric and differential scanning calorimeter analysis
ce:section.ce:para: TGA was employed to investigate the weight ratio of as-prepared samples. Figure S6 showed the process of weight loss as the temperature ranged from 30 °C to 1300 °C. For both samples, the trivial weight loss below 110 °C corresponded to the evaporation of adsorbed-water in the material. In the meantime, in the range of 200 °C–350 °C, a slight weight loss of In2O3/carbon dots (about 3 %) was discerned, which accorded with the DSC curve. A slight weight loss (∼1 %) was also found for the pure In2O3, which could be attributed to the decomposition of residual raw materials. As the temperature rose further, the weight of both samples decreased at a rather slow and almost the same rate, which could be ascribed to the escape of crystal water in the In2O3. As the temperature rose to 1300 °C, the mass of In2O3 and In2O3/carbon dots decreased to 96.3 % and 94.1 % respectively, from which we could conclude that the weight ratio of carbon dots in the composition was 2.2 %.

To prove that the introduction of carbon dots played a positive role in the enhancing of NO2 gas sensing, the gas sensors based on the In2O3 and In2O3/carbon dots were fabricated respectively to investigate the gas sensing properties. It was generally recognized that the operating temperature was a significant criterion for gas sensors, which had great impact on the adsorption/desorption of gas and reaction on the surface. Therefore, responses of as-prepared gas sensors to 500 ppb NO2 were explored in the temperature range of 20 − 120 °C to analyze the temperature-dependent function, and the corresponding consequences were shown in Fig. 6 (a). As can be seen, the response of both sensors displayed an “increase − maximum − decrease” mode with the increase of working temperature. Generally speaking, optimal working temperature could balance activated energy and adsorption/desorption process. High enough activated energy was crucial to overcome the activation energy barrier and stimulate the reaction on the surface. Meanwhile, high temperature would suppress the adsorption of target gas on material [45,46]. For the sensors based on the pristine In2O3 materials, the maximum response to 500 ppb NO2 was 30 at 50 °C. In contrast, for the ones based on the In2O3/carbon dots, the maximum response to 500 ppb NO2 was 130 at the same temperature, which was about 4 times as much as that of the pristine In2O3.

Owing to that the selectivity was also a significant criterion for gas sensors, the selectivity of as-prepared sensors was explored by comparing the response of the both sensors to a series of gas (SO2, Cl2, VOCs, and so on) with same concentration at 50 °C [47,48]. As shown in the Fig. 6(b), for the sensors based on the pristine In2O3, the response to NO2 was interfered by methanol. The sensor based on the pristine In2O3 exhibited nearly half value response to methanol compared with that to NO2, which indicates the the detection of NO2 could be interfered by methanol. In stark contrast, for the sensors based on the In2O3/carbon dots, the response to 500 ppb NO2 was remarkably higher than other gases, which representing an excellent selectivity. In consideration that carbonaceous material usually possessed excellent NO2 sensing properties, e.g., graphene oxide, single-walled and multi-walled carbon nanotube, we think the improved selectivity is mainly attributed to the presence of carbon dots in hybrid.

In addition, the relation between gas concentration and response of sensors was further investigated. Fig. 7 (a) displayed the typical response-recovery curves of sensors based on the In2O3 and In2O3/carbon dots material with NO2 concentration ranging from 50 ppb to 500 ppb at working temperature of 50 °C. As illustrated, the hybrids exhibited n-type semiconductor properties. The resistance in NO2 of both sensors gradually rose with the increase of NO2 concentration. The response of above two sensors in NO2 atmosphere with varied concentration was showed in the Fig. 7(b). With the NO2 concentration increasing, the response of sensors based on the In2O3/carbon dots material rose dramatically higher than that of pristine In2O3, indicating the introduction of carbon dots played a positive role in the enhancing of gas properties. The linear fitting curves of sensor response on NO2 concentration for In2O3 and In2O3/carbon dot are shown in Fig.S7. The theoretical limit of detection was estimated by using standard method (3σ/S). σ represented the standard deviation (SD) and was calculated to be 0.034 and 0.031 for In2O3/carbon dot and In2O3. S represented the slope of sensing curve. Since this method worked for linear response, we restricted our calculation to the linear part (50–200 ppb) as showed in Fig. S7. Therefore the LOD could be calculated to be 1.6 and 4.7 ppb for In2O3/carbon dot and In2O3.

Moreover, the response/recovery characteristic towards 500 ppb NO2 was compared. The response/recovery time of pristine In2O3 was 11.6 min and 5.6 min respectively. In contrast, the response/recovery time of hybrid was 9.6 min and 5.3 min respectively, which indicated that the introduction of carbon dots could lower the response time remarkably. For the decrease of response time, it could be attributed to the high activity of carbon dots surface which can benefit the adsorption of NO2 gas and oxygen molecules. It was noteworthy that the recovery time was mainly determined by the reaction mechanism between the NO2 and the sensing material, hence the decrease of recovery time was not obvious [49].

Further analysis regarding to the sensor stability was carried out. Fig. 8 (a) showed the short-term cycling performance of sensors based on the In2O3/carbon dots hybrid at 50 °C. As can be seen, there was no evident weakening in response to 500 ppb NO2 for five cycle with the baseline almost unchanged throughout, which indicated the excellent steadiness of as-prepared sensors. In addition, continuous sensors response measurement to 500 ppb NO2 was carried out to investigate the long-term stability, pattern of which was displayed in the Fig. 8(b). As illustrated, although the sensor response to 500 ppb NO2 degraded about 30 % during 30 days, the response remain higher than the sensors based on the pristine In2O3. Moreover, the sensor response in different relative humidity atmosphere was measured. As displayed in the Fig. 9 , the sensor response of In2O3/carbon dots decreased with the increase of relative humidity. In 90 % relative humidity atmosphere, the sensor response decreased ∼50 % in contrast to the case in 20 % relative humidity.

To evaluate the sensing performance of as-prepared sensors, a comprehensive comparison involving several crucial indicators (working temperature, response, and detection limit) was carried out, which was intuitively listed in the Table 1 . By comparison, it was quite clear that the as-prepared sensors based on the In2O3/carbon dots possessed outstanding response, relative low working temperature (50 °C) and detection limit (50 ppb). In addition, the present sensors also had excellent stability, which was a crucial criterion in the practical application. Above all, we could come to a conclusion that the sensors based on the In2O3/carbon dots had a promising prospect as a sensing device for NO2 detection.

To further understand the sensing mechanism of In2O3/carbon dots samples, the general sensing mechanism was represented as followed [58,59]. For n-type semiconductor, like In2O3, when exposed in air, the oxygen molecules would be absorbed on the surface of In2O3. These adsorbed oxygen molecules would be ionized by trapping the electron in the conducting band of In2O3 to form chemisorbed oxygen species (
O 2 -
). Afterwards, the electron depletion layer would be created near the surface of In2O3, which would reduce the electron concentration and then increase the resistance. Upon exposure to an oxiding gas, such as NO2, NO2 molecules would trap electron from In2O3 to form
N O
2 -
increased the resistance. The reaction between the adsorbed oxygen species and NO2 was illustrated in Eqs. 1–5 [60–62].
(1) O2(gas) → O2(ads)
(2)
O
2 ( a d s )
+
e -
→
O
2 ( a d s )
-
(3)
N O
2 ( a d s )
+
e -
→
N O
2 ( a d s )
-

Compared to pristine In2O3, the excellent NO2 sensing properties of In2O3/carbon dots hybrid could be attributed to (i) the electron transfer caused by the successful formation of heterojunction between In2O3 and carbon dots, (ii) the shielding effect caused by the introduction of carbon dot, (iii) the increase of percentage Ov and OS induced by the introduction of carbon dots, (iv) high activity of edges on the surface of carbon dots. First of all, the work function of In2O3 was reported to be 4 eV [63], which was significantly lower than the one of carbon dots (5.56 eV) [64], thus the heterojunction would be constructed across the interface of In2O3 and carbon dots, as described in the Fig. 10 . The electron would flow from the In2O3 to the carbon dots until the Femi level was equal. In the meantime, the electron depletion layer would broaden with the bending of energy banding and finally lead to the increase of resistance of In2O3. As reported, the carbon dot could act as efficient electron donor or accepter [34]. The fluorescence of hybrids as mentioned above could also prove the electron transfer. Secondly, the shielding effect caused by the introduction of carbon dot would decrease the effective nuclear charge. Thus, the released electrons would be trapped by the oxygen or nitrogen dioxide molecular and further enhance the sensing property. Furthermore, as discussed in the XPS section, after the introduction of carbon dots, the percentage of the Ov and Os both increase. Owing to that the Os possess higher activity, the increase of percentage of Os was crucial to the enhancement of sensing characteristic. The abundant Ov of crystal structures can offer more free electrons for reaction between adsorbed oxygen and NO2 gas, thus promoting the sensing performance [65,66]·. Finally, the surface of carbon quantum dots possessed ultrahigh chemistry activity, which was beneficial for the adsorption of NO2 gas and oxygen molecules, thus the reaction could be accelerated. Consequently, the introduction of carbon dots would boost the sensing properties of as-prepared sensors owing to its fancy characteristic of physics and chemistry.

Hierarchical In2O3 nanospheres and In2O3/carbon dots hybrid nanospheres were successfully prepared by a facile hydrothermal route. Compared with the pristine hierarchical In2O3 nanospheres, the In2O3/carbon dots heterojunction nanospheres showed much more excellent sensing performance in response, selectivity and detection limit to NO2 gas. After the introduction of carbon dots, the sensing response of sensors to 500 ppb NO2 gas at 50 °C was 130, which was as 4 times as the response of pristine hierarchical In2O3 nanospheres. Furthermore, the theoretical detection limit to NO2 of the In2O3/carbon dots was as low as 9 ppb. The introduction of carbon dots play positive role in sensor response, which were mainly attributed to the construction of heterojunctions between In2O3 and carbon dots, the increase of percentage Ov and OS in hybrid, and high activity of carbon dots. Therefore, we confirm the introduction of carbon quantum dots was a simple, cheap and effective strategy for enhancing the gas sensing performance. We expect that our work can provide reference for development of carbonaceous gas sensor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Nos. 2018M640282, 2019T120238), the National Nature Science Foundation of China (61520106003, 61304242), Program for Chang Jiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (IRT_17R47), Application and Basic Research of Jilin Province (20130102010JC), and STIRT-JLU (2017TD-07), Graduate Interdisciplinary Research Fund of Jilin University (No. 10183201833).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.L. Wang, B. Zhang, J. Liu, Y.Q. Yang, Au-loaded mesoporous WO3: Preparation and n-butanol sensing performances, Sens. Actuators B 236 (2016) 67-76.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ming Cheng received his BE degree from Jilin University of China in 2017. He is currently working toward the MS degree in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. His current researches focus on the preparation and application of graphene and semiconductor oxide, especially in gas sensor.

Zepei Wu received his BE degree from Jilin University of China in 2017. Now he is studying for a MS degree in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. He is interested in the synthesis and application of graphene and semiconductor oxide, especially in gas sensor.

Guannan Liu received his BE degree from Jilin University of China in 2015. He is currently working toward the MS degree in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. His current researches focus on the synthesis of carbon dots and their applications.

Lianjing Zhao received her MS degree from Jilin University of China in 2013. She is currently working toward the Ph.D. degree in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. Her research interests mainly focus on the on the development of the functional nanomaterials for biosensors.

Yuan Gao received her PhD degree from Department of Analytical Chemistry at Jilin University in 2012. Now she is an associate professor in Jilin University, China. Her current researches focus on the preparation and application of graphene and semiconductor oxide, especially in gas sensor and biosensor.

Shan Li is presently studying the MS degree in the Electronics Science and Engineering department, Jilin University. Her research directions involve fabrication of mesoporous oxide semiconductor and their applications in gas sensors.

Bo Zhang received the BE degree in College of Chemistry from Jilin University in 2013. He is currently working toward the Dr. Degree in College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University. His research interests include the synthesis of graphene and its applications in gas sensors.

Xu Yan received his M.S. degree in 2013 from Nanjing Agricultural University. He joined the group of Prof. Xingguang Su at Jilin University and received his Ph.D. degree in June 2017. Since then, he did postdoctoral work with Prof. Geyu Lu. Currently, his research interests mainly focus on the development of the functional nanomaterials for chem/bio sensors.

Geyu Lu received the B. Sci. degree in electronic sciences in 1985 and the M. Sci.degree in 1988 from Jilin University in China and the Dr. Eng. degree in 1998 from Kyushu University in Japan. Now he is a professor of Jilin University, China. His current research interests include the development of chemical sensors and the application of the function materials.
